result:
The city had a dozen public clocks and one person who kept them honest. Mara carried a ring of keys that chimed faintly when she walked, a small bottle of graphite, a rag that always smelled like oranges, and a notebook where she logged what each clock believed about the world.

People liked to say time was the same for everyone. You set a device and it synced to a tower and that was the end of it. But concrete swells and shrinks with the weather, housings settle, gears consider their age. Pigeons roost where vibration matters. Wind sneaks into hairline seams. Each clock wore its own biography of scratches and dents, and each one leaned a little toward the truth it preferred. Mara did not impose; she persuaded. She would set her ear to a case, count the nails rattling in something unseen, and murmur, You and me both. Let’s be reasonable.

At the library, the clock in the atrium swore by the management of whispers and dust. It wanted to lag half a minute behind, as if to protect the hush by prolonging it. The one above the fish market ran sharper in the mornings, galvanized by the urgency of ice and knives, then softened after lunch when salt and gulls had done their work. The tram station clock had a cough that came and went with humidity. She had tried fixing that one twice, and finally left the cough alone; some clocks earned their right to be idiosyncratic.

Her notes were precise because the clocks were not. North Chapel—gains 3 seconds/day (winter), loses 1 (summer)—check weathervane. Municipal Annex—second hand sticks at 52 when west wind is high. She kept drawings of gear teeth that were almost right and arrows that reminded her where to be gentle. Her handwriting swerved between confident and spidery. On cold days her knuckles were stiff; those entries looked like someone had written with a twig.

Most days narrowed to a rhythm of ladders and light. Up a stair, through a door, across a catwalk, keys between her fingers, the familiar resistance of an old lock, the way a mechanism responded to a touch as if it had been waiting for just that long. Sometimes a pigeon would launch near her face and she would swear without malice. Sometimes she would find a note tucked into a housing—Please make this one faster, I am always late—or a child’s drawing of the clock with wild sun-rays of hands. She saved the drawings and pretended the pleas had not struck her.

Trouble announced itself in thirteens. The schoolyard clock on Telling Street—named, incongruously, after an inventor of umbrellas—leapt ahead by thirteen minutes overnight, twice in one week. Mara checked the usual suspects. Loose weight? No. Bent pallet? No. Was something stealing heat from the pendulum in the early hours? The casing was intact, a little nicked where someone had jammed it in the past with a ruler, but otherwise fine. She adjusted, cursed kindly, tightened what could be tightened, dabbed a whisper of oil where friction had turned hopeful. She had her coffee on the steps and waited. Birds wrote lineless alphabets overhead. The clock behaved, chastened, until the following dawn when it had made up its mind again and run.

She logged everything, even her confusion. Gains 13 at night. Check tram schedule. Talk to Mr. Hsu the groundskeeper. Notice the light on the leaves at 6:30; it made the fence look complicated.

If a thing can be haunted without tragedy, the schoolyard clock was haunted by purpose. At two-thirty in the morning, Mara walked to Telling Street with a flashlight the size of a rolling pin. The air had the blank quality of late-night air, where sound seems embarrassed to exist. She sat on the low wall and listened to the faint metallic talk inside the case as the clock argued with itself. Across the street, a window was lit. Someone moved past it once, twice.

After a while, an older woman came to sit on the other end of the wall. She had a small suitcase: the kind made with a front pocket nobody uses. Her hands were folded on its handle. She glanced at Mara’s keys, at the enormous flashlight, and smiled the way you do at someone doing a job at an hour you didn’t expect to see it done.

You work the clocks? she asked, as though the clocks were a crop you harvested.

Try to, Mara said.

They run fast around here, said the woman, as if announcing a local allergy. You can feel it.

Mara squinted at the mechanism through the glass of the face. The second hand moved as if someone had told it good news. She heard, far beneath, the sub-basement throb of the city’s power. She looked at the suitcase.

Bus comes at five, the woman said. My sister’s in the next city over, the one with the river that never makes up its mind. We haven’t spoken in fifteen years. I don’t know if she’ll want to see me. I don’t know if I want to see her. I sat in my apartment until I thought about morning the way you think about rain when you need it, and then I thought maybe if I came here, it would hurry up for me.

Mara considered all the forces her notebook accounted for: thermal expansion, gear lash, the insistent dust of playgrounds. She had never made room in a margin for this. The woman’s heart beat fast. The suitcase clicked where its hinge was not sure. Somewhere a baker was proofing dough for rolls whose smell would set the hour more firmly than sunlight ever could.

She unlocked the case and watched the pendulum. It swung with a zeal that was almost comic. She placed two fingers on the housing to feel the echo along the wood. Her watch, radio-synced and bulldog-accurate, told her what the second hand should do. The hand sprinted more than it walked.

You shouldn’t do that, Mara said to the clock without embarrassment. We’ll be sorry later.

But she did not change it. The woman on the wall was breathing like someone whose ribs were each remembering their job.

When morning finally came, it did so all at once, as it always does if you happen to be watching. The woman stood and picked up her suitcase. You don’t have to fix all of them, she said, as if finishing a conversation neither had started. She crossed the street toward the stop where the first light fell and washed the advertisement there until it looked like a painting of a bright rectangle.

After she left, Mara slowed the clock. She set the pendulum where she would have liked it to be in a world that did not include longing as a variable. She waited a day. The clock kept its agreement. The following night it ran ahead again, not thirteen minutes this time but nine. There was no suitcase on the wall, but a boy paced under the awning of the corner shop with a bouquet that looked like he had given up and bought everything red.

Over the course of a week, Mara watched the clock take sides. When someone waited with intention, the minute hand leaned toward them. When the street was empty of desire, the clock remembered its parameters. She adjusted carefully, splitting differences, documenting whims. Her notebook picked up a new symbol: a small arrow that meant hope, an annotation even she would find silly to explain.

She began to make other, deliberate allowances. In the hospital corridor where waiting was inevitable and counted breath by breath, she left the wall clock one minute fast. In the playground, she gave children an extra soft minute before they were called in. Above the factory where the shift changed in a river of people at midnight, she set the face in a hard friendship with the whistle that blew them out. She did not bend time so much as show it where to be kind.

Once, while tightening the brass clip on the cathedral’s pendulum, she saw her own reflection in the curve of it. She looked like someone who both belonged inside machines and had never quite been meant to. She smiled at herself and thought of the woman on Telling Street on a bench that had made itself into a threshold.

It turned out tending time was less about sternness than about listening to where the city pressed its weight. She learned to hear the frequency of need. The fish market never asked for leniency, only precision and mercy in the same measure. The library wanted not seconds but tone. The schoolyard wanted the grace of seconds falling like chalk dust.

On certain dawns, with her ring of keys lighter for being used, Mara would walk past the tram station and the baker and the echoing glass of the pharmacy windows, and she would feel, for a brief and private instant, all the clocks inhale. The city made a sound like lungs. She answered it by winding, by wiping, by allowing, by aligning. Every day she came a little closer to the truth that time in a place is made as much by people as by pendulums, and that the work is not to correct it into sameness but to lean with it until it finds what’s fair.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
